content:
4 min read
3 hours ago

--

If you’ve recently connected a React (Vite) frontend with a Spring Boot backend, you’ve probably encountered an error like this:
Access to XMLHttpRequest at 'http://localhost:8080/saveUser'
from origin 'http://localhost:5174'
has been blocked by CORS policy:Response to preflight request doesn't pass access control check:
No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present on the requested resource.Or you may also see:
Failed to load resource: net::ERR_FAILEDThis is one of the most common issues developers face when the frontend and backend are running on different ports.
In this article, I’ll explain:

• What CORS is

• Why this error occurs

• What a preflight request is

• How to fix it in Spring Boot

• Additional configuration required when using Spring Security

Understanding the Problem
Suppose your applications are running as follows:
Frontend (React + Vite)
http://localhost:5174Backend (Spring Boot)
http://localhost:8080Although both applications are running on your local machine, they are considered different origins because they use different ports.
When your frontend sends a request like:
POST http://localhost:8080/saveUserthe browser first checks whether the backend allows requests from another origin.
If the backend does not return the required CORS headers, the browser blocks the request before it even reaches your controller.
That’s why you see the following error:
No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present on the requested resource.
What Is CORS?
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism that controls how web applications access resources hosted on different origins.
An origin consists of:

• Protocol (HTTP/HTTPS)

• Domain

• Port
For example:
http://localhost:5174and
http://localhost:8080are different origins because they use different ports.
By default, browsers block cross-origin requests unless the server explicitly allows them.

Why Does the Browser Send a Preflight Request?
Before sending certain requests (such as POST, PUT, DELETE, or requests with custom headers), the browser first sends an OPTIONS request called a preflight request.
Its purpose is to ask the server:
“Are you okay with this request coming from another origin?”
If the server responds with the appropriate CORS headers, the browser proceeds with the actual request.
Otherwise, it blocks the request and displays the CORS error.

Solution 1: Enable CORS for a Specific Controller
The easiest approach is to use Spring’s @CrossOrigin annotation.
@CrossOrigin(origins = "http://localhost:5174")
@RestController
public class UserController { @PostMapping("/saveUser")
public ResponseEntity<String> saveUser(@RequestBody User user) { // Save user return ResponseEntity.ok("User saved successfully");
}
}This allows requests only from your React application.

Solution 2: Configure CORS Globally (Recommended)
If your application contains multiple controllers, configuring CORS globally is a better approach.
Create a Configuration Class
File Name
WebConfig.javaRecommended Location
src
└── main
└── java
└── com
└── example
└── demo
├── DemoApplication.java
└── config
└── WebConfig.javaComplete Configuration
package com.example.demo.config;import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;
import org.springframework.web.servlet.config.annotation.CorsRegistry;
import org.springframework.web.servlet.config.annotation.WebMvcConfigurer;@Configuration
public class WebConfig { @Bean
public WebMvcConfigurer corsConfigurer() { return new WebMvcConfigurer() { @Override
public void addCorsMappings(CorsRegistry registry) { registry.addMapping("/**")
.allowedOrigins("http://localhost:5174")
.allowedMethods(
"GET",
"POST",
"PUT",
"DELETE",
"OPTIONS")
.allowedHeaders("*")
.allowCredentials(true);
}
};
}
}This configuration enables CORS for all endpoints in your application.

Understanding the Configuration
@Configuration
Marks the class as a Spring configuration class.
WebMvcConfigurer
Allows customization of Spring MVC behavior.
addCorsMappings()
Registers CORS rules.
addMapping("/**")
Applies the configuration to every endpoint.
Examples:

• /saveUser

• /login

• /register

• /products
allowedOrigins("http://localhost:5174")
Only allows requests from your React application.
allowedMethods(...)
Specifies the HTTP methods that clients are allowed to use.
allowedHeaders("*")
Allows all request headers.
allowCredentials(true)
Allows cookies and authentication headers to be included in requests.

If You’re Using Spring Security
Many developers enable CORS in WebConfig but still encounter the same error.
The reason is that Spring Security has its own filter chain, and CORS must also be enabled there.
Enable CORS in your security configuration:
http
.cors()
.and()
.csrf()
.disable();Also define a CorsConfigurationSource bean:
@Bean
public CorsConfigurationSource corsConfigurationSource() { CorsConfiguration config = new CorsConfiguration(); config.setAllowedOrigins(List.of("http://localhost:5174"));
config.setAllowedMethods(
List.of("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS"));
config.setAllowedHeaders(List.of("*"));
config.setAllowCredentials(true); UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource source =
new UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource(); source.registerCorsConfiguration("/**", config); return source;
}Without this configuration, Spring Security may reject the preflight request before it reaches your controller.

Common Mistakes
Avoid these common pitfalls:

• Don’t use * as the allowed origin when allowCredentials(true) is enabled.

• Make sure your backend handles OPTIONS requests.

• Ensure the frontend URL exactly matches the configured origin.

• Verify that the backend is actually running on the expected port.

• Restart your Spring Boot application after updating the configuration.

Quick Debug Checklist
When troubleshooting CORS issues, verify the following:

• Is your backend running?

• Does the backend respond to OPTIONS /saveUser?

• Does the response include:
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:5174Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, OPTIONS
• Is the frontend sending requests to the correct URL?

• Are Spring Security settings configured properly?

Production Considerations
During development, your frontend may run on:
http://localhost:5174However, in production you should restrict CORS to your actual frontend domain:
.allowedOrigins("https://your-production-domain.com")Avoid allowing every origin in production, as it can introduce security risks.

Conclusion
CORS errors can be frustrating, especially when you’re just trying to connect your frontend and backend. Fortunately, the solution is straightforward once you understand how browsers enforce cross-origin security.
To resolve the issue:

• Enable CORS in your Spring Boot application.

• Configure Spring Security if you’re using it.

• Ensure the server responds correctly to preflight (OPTIONS) requests.

• Verify the required Access-Control-Allow-* headers are included in the response.
Once these configurations are in place, your React frontend will be able to communicate with your Spring Boot backend without encountering CORS-related errors.
I hope this guide helps you solve the issue quickly. Happy coding!
